title: Anticoagulant Therapy: Heparin and Warfarin
subtitle: The nurse manages anticoagulants — monitoring aPTT for heparin (antidote protamine) and INR for warfarin (antidote vitamin K), teaching bleeding precautions, and watching for signs of bleeding.

patient.name: Anticoagulant Therapy
patient.admitDate: The nurse safely manages a patient receiving anticoagulant therapy.

nursesNote: The nurse safely manages anticoagulant therapy, which prevents clot formation and extension but raises bleeding risk. Heparin acts quickly (IV or SQ) and is monitored with the aPTT (therapeutic about 1.5-2.5 times baseline; anti-Xa may also be used); its antidote is protamine sulfate. Warfarin is oral with a slow onset over several days and is monitored with the INR (usual goal 2-3; an INR above 4 signals high bleeding risk); its antidote is vitamin K, so the patient keeps dietary vitamin K (leafy greens) steady rather than suddenly bingeing or avoiding it. Low-molecular-weight heparin such as enoxaparin is given SQ and usually needs no routine lab monitoring. The nurse watches for any signs of bleeding (bruising, bleeding gums, blood in urine or stool, prolonged bleeding) and monitors platelets for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT), stopping heparin if it develops. Bleeding precautions are taught: a soft toothbrush, an electric razor, avoiding aspirin/NSAIDs unless ordered, fall prevention, and reporting bleeding. Two anticoagulants are high-alert drugs requiring an independent double-check, and IM injections are generally avoided.
providerOrders: Monitor aPTT for heparin and keep protamine available
Monitor INR for warfarin and keep vitamin K available
Teach the patient to keep dietary vitamin K intake steady
Assess for bleeding and report bruising, blood in urine/stool, or bleeding gums
Teach bleeding precautions: soft toothbrush, electric razor, avoid NSAIDs

opts.text: Heparin therapy is monitored with the aPTT
Warfarin therapy is monitored with the INR
Protamine sulfate reverses heparin
Anticoagulants dissolve existing clots quickly
Vitamin K reverses warfarin
The nurse watches for signs of bleeding
An INR above 4 means a high risk of bleeding
The patient keeps dietary vitamin K intake steady
Aspirin and NSAIDs add to bleeding risk
Heparin and warfarin are high-alert medications
A rising platelet count suggests HIT
Enoxaparin usually needs no routine monitoring

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order starting an IV heparin drip

opts.text: Verify the order and a baseline aPTT/platelets
Do an independent two-nurse high-alert check
Start the infusion per protocol with protamine available
Monitor aPTT and titrate to the therapeutic range
Assess for bleeding and document
Monitored with the aPTT
Monitored with the INR
Antidote is protamine sulfate
Antidote is vitamin K
Oral drug with a slow onset over days
Dietary leafy greens affect the drug's effect

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A warfarin patient has INR 6 with bleeding gums
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Hold the warfarin dose
Assess for other signs of bleeding
Notify the provider of the INR and bleeding
Give vitamin K if ordered
Document and continue monitoring the INR
Using a soft toothbrush and electric razor
Taking aspirin for a headache without asking
Keeping leafy-green intake about the same daily
Doubling the warfarin dose for a missed dose
Reporting blood in the urine or stool
Giving heparin by deep IM injection
Keeping protamine available during a heparin drip
Ignoring a falling platelet count on heparin
